docs.title: JSim
docs.text:
JSim is a modular, open-source physics simulation library focused on FRC robotics.

This documentation provides onboarding, API usage, architecture details, and reference material for simulation, math, and CAD workflows.

docs.title: Quick Links
docs.text:
• Getting Started: getting_started.md
  • API Usage: api_usage.md
  • Architecture: architecture.md
  • Physics Reference: physics_reference.md
  • Math Reference: math_index.md

docs.title: Contributing
docs.text: See the repository CONTRIBUTING.md in the project root for contribution guidelines, and open issues or pull requests on GitHub.

docs.title: Feedback
docs.text: If something is missing or unclear, please file an issue at https://github.com/Ruthie-FRC/JSim/issues

docs.title: JSim
docs.text:
JSim is a modular FRC physics library for simulation, analysis, and robotics workflow integration.

Project Status

JSim is under active development. Core simulation features and additional capabilities are being added iteratively.

docs.title: Why JSim
docs.text:
• Unified physics engine for rigid-body, drivetrain, and mechanism simulation
  • Multi-language support with native C++ and Python/Java bindings
  • Simulation runtime components for robot code and sensor pipelines
  • CAD import workflows for URDF, STL, and related geometry formats
  • Modular architecture for custom models, forces, and plugins

docs.title: Project Goals
docs.text:
• Accuracy: physically grounded simulation for FRC robots and game elements
  • Integration: seamless workflow from CAD to code to simulation
  • Accessibility: practical for teams of varied experience levels
  • Open source: community-driven and MIT-licensed

docs.title: Build and Test
docs.text: scripts/run-tests.sh

docs.title: Direct Gradle Workflow
docs.text:
cd vendordep
./gradlew test

scripts/run-tests.sh is the recommended path from repository root and expects Java 21.

docs.title: Run Examples
docs.text:
• C++: examples/cpp/minimal_world.cpp
  • Python: examples/python/simple_world_demo.py
  • Java: examples/java/FlywheelPredictionExample.java

docs.title: Documentation Map
docs.text:
• API Usage: integrating JSim into applications
  • Java API Reference (Javadocs): generated Java bindings and APIs
  • Native API Reference (Doxygen): generated C++ and mixed-language symbols
  • Architecture: subsystem layout and extension model
  • Physics Reference: models, assumptions, and equations
  • Math Overview: math modules and conventions
  • Integrators: numerical integration methods
  • Vector3, Matrix3, Quaternion: core math primitives
  • Units and Conventions: coordinate and unit standards

docs.title: Community and Contribution
docs.text:
Contributions are welcome from teams, mentors, and developers across the FRC ecosystem.

  • Report bugs and request features in GitHub Issues
  • Propose improvements through pull requests
  • Follow project standards in the repository documentation

docs.title: Aerodynamics
docs.text:
Aerodynamic force approximations used in JSim.

Topics - Lift and drag coefficients - Reference area and dynamic pressure

Usage - Aerodynamic forces are applied per-body and can be configured per-model.

docs.title: API Usage
docs.text:
This page describes how to use JSim from the supported languages and runtime layers.

C++ (native) - Link against the native library built in core/build or via the top-level build system. - Include headers from core/include and link libjsim.

Python - Use the Python bindings (generated under python/). - Example: import the bindings in examples/python/simple_world_demo.py.

Java - Use the Java bindings in jsim/java/ or the generated JAR/Javadoc artifacts.

Common patterns - Create a world, add bodies/mechanisms, step simulation with a fixed timestep, and collect state for logging or telemetry. - Use the sensor_pipeline components in apps/sim-runtime/ for example integrations.

docs.title: Architecture
docs.text:
JSim is organized into layered subsystems:

  • Core physics engine: numerical integrators, collision handling, contacts
  • Models: drivetrain, mechanisms, aerodynamics, ball and field elements
  • IO and CAD: URDF/STL import, material definitions
  • Language bindings: Python, Java, and native C++ APIs
  • Examples and runtime: robotics runtime, sensor pipeline, and sample robots

Extension points - Add new models by implementing the model interface in cad-import/mechanisms.py or C++ equivalents. - Bindings are generated from the native API; update python/bindings.cpp and build scripts to expose new functions.

docs.title: Collision Impulses
docs.text:
JSim uses an impulse-based collision model for resolving penetrations and contacts.

Key points - Impulses are computed using relative velocities, effective mass, restitution, and friction. - Contacts may be resolved with sequential impulses or a constraint solver depending on the scenario.

For deep dives, examine the core solver implementation in core/src.

docs.title: Drivetrain & Mechanism Dynamics
docs.text:
This page summarizes models used for drivetrain and mechanism simulation.

Drivetrain - Differential drive: wheel-ground interaction, slip, and torque limits - Swerve/mecanum: kinematics and per-wheel friction models (when implemented)

Mechanisms - Gear trains, belt-driven elements, and attached subsystems modeled with joint limits and actuator maps.

Tuneable parameters - Motor models, gearbox ratios, friction, and damping values are exposed in model constructors.

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text:
This page helps you build and run examples for JSim.

Prerequisites - Java 17+ (or the version the project targets) - C++ toolchain (clang/gcc) for native builds - Python 3.8+ for Python examples

Quick build (recommended)

scripts/run-tests.sh

Run a single example (Python)

python3 examples/python/simple_world_demo.py

Running Java examples

Use Gradle in vendordep or the top-level Gradle wrapper:

./gradlew :examples:java:run

Where to go next - API Usage: api_usage.md - Examples: check examples/ and jsim/examples/ for language-specific samples

docs.title: Integrators
docs.text:
Supported numerical integrators and guidance:

  • Explicit Euler: simple, fast, but unstable for stiff systems
  • Semi-implicit (symplectic) Euler: better energy behaviour for many robotics systems
  • Runge–Kutta 4 (RK4): higher accuracy, more expensive — useful for offline accuracy checks

Recommendation: use fixed timestep with semi-implicit Euler for real-time simulations; use RK4 for verification.

docs.title: Magnus Effect
docs.text:
The Magnus effect model applies a lift force proportional to spin and relative velocity for spinning spherical bodies.

Parameters - Spin vector, Magnus coefficient, reference radius

docs.title: Math Reference
docs.text:
This section collects the math primitives and conventions used across JSim.

  • Vector3: vector operations and utilities — see vector.md
  • Matrix3: rotation and inertia operations — see matrix.md
  • Quaternion: orientation math and slerp — see quaternion.md
  • Integrators: numerical integrators and recommended use — see integrators.md

docs.title: Matrix3
docs.text:
3x3 matrix utilities used for rotations, inertia tensors, and transforms.

Examples

R = Matrix3.from_axis_angle(axis, angle)
I_world = R * I_body * R.transpose()

docs.title: Physics Models
docs.text:
Overview of high-level physical models provided by JSim.

  • Rigid-body dynamics: Mass, inertia, external forces, constraint solving
  • Drivetrain models: differential, swerve, and mecanum abstractions (where implemented)
  • Mechanism elements: gears, belts, attached subsystems
  • Ball and field interactions: bounce, friction, and contact geometry

See physics_reference.md for assumptions and derivations.

docs.title: Physics Reference
docs.text:
This page lists the core assumptions and equations used by JSim.

Coordinate frames and units - Right-handed coordinates are used unless otherwise noted. - SI units are preferred: meters, seconds, kilograms, radians.

Equations - Motion: Newton–Euler equations for rigid bodies - Contacts: impulse-based collision resolution with restitution and friction models

Numerical notes - Timestepping choices and integrator stability affect energy conservation and drift. See integrators.md.

docs.title: Quaternion
docs.text:
Quaternion math for representing orientations and smooth interpolation.

Examples

q = Quaternion.from_axis_angle(axis, angle)
q_slerp = Quaternion.slerp(q0, q1, t)

docs.title: Season Authoring
docs.text:
Guidance for creating and authoring field layouts, presets, and season assets.

See cad-import/FIELD_RECREATION_GUIDE.md for field recreation steps and examples.

docs.title: Units and Conventions
docs.text:
• Units: SI (m, s, kg, rad)
  • Coordinate system: right-handed, +X forward, +Y right, +Z up (project-wide convention)
  • Angles: radians unless labeled degrees
  • Time step: simulations typically use fixed timesteps; tune integrator settings for stability

docs.title: Vector3
docs.text:
Core vector utilities for positions, velocities, and forces.

Examples

v = Vector3(1, 0, 0)
v.rotate(axis, theta)

See code in cad-import/geometry_utils.py for Python helpers.

docs.title: Annotated Classes — API Guide
docs.text:
This page summarizes the project's core class concepts and how to find and use annotated classes in the source.

Overview - The codebase is organized into a native C++ core, CAD import and model layers, and language bindings. Core classes represent simulation state (world, bodies, constraints), model definitions (drivetrains, mechanisms), and helpers (math primitives, integrators).

Where to find classes - Native C++: see core/src for the core simulation types and solvers. - Python helpers and wrappers: see python/ and examples/python/simple_world_demo.py for usage patterns. - Java examples and bindings: see jsim/examples and jsim/java.

How to use - Instantiate a simulation "world", create bodies or model instances, configure their properties (mass, inertia, joints), and advance the simulation with a fixed timestep. - Collect state (pose, velocity) each step and feed it to logging, telemetry, or a robot runtime.

Notes - This page is a human-oriented summary; for API reference consult the implementation files in core/src and the example programs in examples/ for concrete call patterns.

docs.title: Class Member Enumerations — Guide
docs.text: Usage - Classes may contain enums to represent mode selections or flags scoped to the class. Inspect the class header for the exact enumeration values and semantics.

docs.title: Class Member Functions — Guide
docs.text:
What to expect - Member functions implement behavior for simulation objects: stepping, applying forces, querying state, and configuring properties.

Inspecting implementations - Find definitions in core/src and corresponding headers in core/include to understand parameter semantics and lifetime expectations.

docs.title: Class Member Typedefs — Guide
docs.text: Purpose - Typedefs inside classes typically expose commonly used internal types (e.g., vector or iterator types) that help with API ergonomics.

docs.title: Class Member Variables — Guide
docs.text: Notes - Member variables typically represent configuration or state; prefer private members with public accessors to preserve invariants.

docs.title: Class Members — Guide
docs.text:
Overview - Class members include data fields, methods, nested types, and inner enums. For simulation objects, members typically expose configuration and current state (position, velocity).

Best practices - Prefer accessor methods instead of exposing mutable public fields.

docs.title: Classes — Key API Concepts
docs.text:
Core concepts - Simulation objects are represented by classes grouped by purpose: world/simulation containers, physical bodies, joints/mechanisms, sensors, and model builders.

Learning by example - The examples/ folders show how to instantiate and use classes across languages. Use examples/python/simple_world_demo.py as a quick reference for typical usage.

docs.title: Files & Source Locations — Guide
docs.text:
Where to read implementation - Core engine: core/src and core/include - CAD import and materials: cad-import/ - Examples and runtime: examples/, apps/, and jsim/examples

Tip - Read the example that is closest to your intended use case, then follow symbols into the implementation for details.

docs.title: Functions — Usage Guide
docs.text:
Common function categories - Creation/factory functions: create worlds, parsers, and model builders. - Math helpers: vector/matrix/quaternion operations are provided in the math modules. - IO functions: loaders for URDF/STL, exporters, and material parsers.

Examples - See examples/python/simple_world_demo.py to observe a complete flow: instantiate, configure, step, record.

docs.title: Class Hierarchy — Understanding Type Relationships
docs.text:
Why hierarchy matters - Understanding inheritance and composition helps when extending or customizing models.

How to discover hierarchy - Look at header files in core/include to see base classes and derived types. Examples illustrate which classes to extend for custom behavior.

docs.title: Links
docs.text:
# API Guide — Cross-links

This collection of pages provides human-readable guidance for the codebase API. Instead of linking to generated API artifacts, each page explains where to find implementation and usage examples in the repository and how to apply the API in C++, Python, and Java.

Key locations - Core implementation: core/src, core/include - CAD import: cad-import - Python bindings and examples: python/ and examples/python - Java bindings and examples: jsim/java and jsim/examples

View the generated API under /api/ in the site output.

docs.title: Macros & Compile-time Helpers — Guide
docs.text:
Where macros are used - Macros are primarily used for header guards, conditional compilation, and small helper macros in the native C++ code under core/include and python/ binding glue.

Advice - Prefer functions or inline templates for functionality; reserve macros for preprocessor-level concerns.

docs.title: Modules — API Guide
docs.text:
Concept - The repository is arranged in logical modules: core (physics kernel), cad-import (geometry, URDF/STL handling), apps (runtimes and examples), python and jsim/java (bindings and examples).

Typical module responsibilities - core: integrators, collision, constraints, and low-level solvers. - cad-import: parsing and converting CAD artifacts to runtime geometry. - apps: example robot runtimes, sensor pipelines, and robot loaders.

Using modules - Build the whole project with the top-level build scripts. Use examples in each module to learn API usage: examples/cpp, examples/python, jsim/examples.

docs.title: Namespace Enumerations — Guide
docs.text:
Enumerations - Enums are used for selecting solver modes, collision settings, or material flags. Inspect core/include headers to see named enums and their meanings.

Best practice - Prefer using named enums from headers for clarity; avoid magic numbers in code.

docs.title: Namespace Functions — Guide
docs.text:
Common functions - Utility functions live next to the math and geometry helpers (see cad-import/geometry_utils.py). - Factory and parser functions for URDF/STL live in cad-import/importer.py and related modules.

How to learn them - Inspect example usage in examples/ and in cad-import/examples, then open the implementation files to review parameter lists and return types.

docs.title: Typedefs & Type Aliases — Guide
docs.text:
Overview - Type aliases simplify complex template or vector types. Look for using or typedef declarations in core/include headers.

Recommendation - Use aliases that improve readability in your code; inspect existing aliases for common patterns.

docs.title: Namespace Variables — Guide
docs.text:
Constants and global configuration - Global constants and configuration flags (e.g., default timestep, gravity) are declared in header files in core/include or module-level config files in cad-import.

Changing defaults - Prefer passing configuration via constructors or configuration objects rather than mutating globals at runtime.

docs.title: Namespace Members — Guide
docs.text:
What they are - Namespace members include free functions, enums, typedefs, and constants used across modules. They are grouped by subsystem in the source tree.

Finding members - Use your editor's search to find symbols. The tests/ and examples/ directories demonstrate how many namespace members are intended to be used.

docs.title: Namespaces & Packages — API Guide
docs.text:
Overview - The native code uses C++ namespaces to separate subsystems. The Python and Java bindings expose idiomatic modules/packages corresponding to these namespaces.

Where to look - C++ namespaces: core/src and cad-import source files. - Python: the python/ package exposes helpers and glue code. - Java: jsim/java contains Java package layout and examples.

Practical tip - Search for the symbol you need (class or function name) in the source tree to find the namespace and the header/source pair to inspect implementation and usage.

docs.title: API Pages — Guide
docs.text: This directory contains human-oriented API pages and guidance. Use these pages if you need to understand where to find types, functions, and example usage in the source tree.

docs.title: API Variables & Constants — Guide
docs.text:
Common uses - Constants for physical defaults (gravity, standard friction) and build-time version strings are defined in headers and top-level config files.

Finding definitions - Search core/include and top-level configuration files to locate declarations and default values.
